response:
THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY GORDON MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROPROCESSOR WILL DOUBLE ABOUT EVERY TWO YEARS. THIS TENDENCY HAS DRIVEN THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING POWER AND TECHNOLOGY OVER THE PAST DECADES. HOWEVER, AS TECHNOLOGY HAS PROGRESSED, PHYSICAL LIMITATIONS HAVE BECOME MORE PROMINENT, SUCH AS HEAT DISSIPATION, QUANTUM TUNNELLING, AND THE MINIMUM PHYSICAL SIZE OF TRANSISTORS. THESE FACTORS HAVE MADE IT INCREASINGLY DIFFICULT TO CONTINUE THE SAME RATE OF PROGRESSION, LEADING TO SPECULATION ABOUT WHEN THE LAW WILL CEASE TO BE VALID.

***  
THE LAW OF MOORE IS NOT A PHYSICAL LAW BUT A TRENDS OBSERVATION, SO IT IS NOT UNCONDITIONALLY VALID. AS MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES HAVE EVOLVED, THE RATE OF IMPROVEMENT HAS SLOWED, AND NEW APPROACHES SUCH AS 3D CHIP ARCHITECTURES, QUANTUM COMPUTING, AND ADVANCED MATERIALS ARE BEING EXPLORED TO EXTEND THE LAW’S RELEVANCE. WHILE IT IS DIFFICULT TO PREDICT EXACTLY WHEN THE LAW WILL STOP APPLYING, IT IS LIKELY THAT IT WILL CONTINUE TO INFLUENCE TECHNOLOGY FOR SOME TIME TO COME. DO
